mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and operation of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is installed via the clusterctl CLI (not Helm). CAPI is a Kubernetes SIG project used by platform operators to automate cluster lifecycle management across various infrastructure environments.

steps.title: Install the clusterctl CLI
steps.description:
Download and install the clusterctl CLI, which is the primary tool for managing Cluster API.

**Linux (AMD64):**
```bash
curl -L https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/clusterctl-linux-amd64 -o clusterctl
sudo install -o root -g root -m 0755 clusterctl /usr/local/bin/clusterctl
clusterctl version
```

**macOS (Homebrew):**
```bash
brew install clusterctl
clusterctl version
```

> Verify the output shows v1.12.4 or later.

steps.title: Initialize the management cluster
steps.description:
Transform your existing Kubernetes cluster into a CAPI management cluster. This installs the core CAPI components, cert-manager, and an infrastructure provider.

```bash
clusterctl init --infrastructure <provider>
```

Replace `<provider>` with your infrastructure: `docker` (dev/test), `aws`, `azure`, `vsphere`, `gcp`, `openstack`, or `metal3`.

Example for AWS:
```bash
export AWS_B64ENCODED_CREDENTIALS=$(clusterawsadm bootstrap credentials encode-as-profile)
clusterctl init --infrastructure aws
```

> This installs: cluster-api core, kubeadm bootstrap provider, kubeadm control-plane provider, cert-manager, and the specified infrastructure provider.

steps.title: Verify CAPI components are running
steps.description:
Check that all CAPI controller pods are running:

```bash
kubectl get pods -A | grep -E 'capi-|caip-|cert-manager'
clusterctl describe --show-conditions all
```

> You should see pods running in namespaces like capi-system, capi-kubeadm-bootstrap-system, and capi-kubeadm-control-plane-system.

steps.title: Generate and create a workload cluster
steps.description:
Use clusterctl to generate a workload cluster manifest, then apply it:

```bash
clusterctl generate cluster my-cluster --kubernetes-version v1.31.0 --control-plane-machine-count 1 --worker-machine-count 3 > my-cluster.yaml
kubectl apply -f my-cluster.yaml
```

> Review the generated YAML before applying. It creates Cluster, Machine, and infrastructure-specific resources.
commands: clusterctl generate cluster my-cluster --kubernetes-version v1.31.0 --control-plane-machine-count 1 --worker-machine-count 3 > my-cluster.yaml

steps.title: Verify the workload cluster
steps.description:
Monitor the cluster provisioning and get the kubeconfig when ready:

```bash
kubectl get clusters
clusterctl describe cluster my-cluster
clusterctl get kubeconfig my-cluster > my-cluster.kubeconfig
kubectl --kubeconfig=my-cluster.kubeconfig get nodes
```

> The cluster status should show 'Provisioned' and all nodes should be 'Ready'.

uninstall.title: Delete workload clusters and uninstall CAPI
uninstall.description:
First delete any workload clusters, then remove CAPI components:

```bash
kubectl delete cluster my-cluster
clusterctl delete --all
```

> This removes all CAPI providers and components. Workload clusters must be deleted first to avoid orphaned infrastructure resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade CAPI components
upgrade.description:
Use clusterctl to upgrade all providers to a new version:

```bash
clusterctl upgrade plan
clusterctl upgrade apply --contract v1beta1
```

> The upgrade plan shows available upgrades. The apply command upgrades all providers to the latest version supporting the v1beta1 API contract.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to get provider components'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The infrastructure provider may not be configured correctly or credentials are missing.

**Fix:**
Check provider-specific environment variables (e.g., AWS_B64ENCODED_CREDENTIALS for AWS). See the provider's documentation for required credentials.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Infrastructure provisioning may still be in progress or has failed.

**Fix:**
Run `clusterctl describe cluster <name> --show-conditions all` to see detailed status and conditions.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** CAPI CRDs are not installed.

**Fix:**
Run `clusterctl init` to install the core CAPI components including CRDs.

troubleshooting.title: cert-manager pods not starting
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** cert-manager may conflict with an existing installation.

**Fix:**
If cert-manager is already installed, use `clusterctl init --cert-manager-timeout 0` to skip cert-manager installation.

versionNotes.changes: Bug fixes and stability improvements. Supports Kubernetes 1.28-1.31.
versionNotes.deprecations: v1alpha3 and v1alpha4 API versions have been removed. All resources use v1beta1.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.6.x or earlier, run 'clusterctl upgrade apply --contract v1beta1' to migrate all resources.

resolution.summary: Run 'clusterctl init --infrastructure <provider>' to set up CAPI, then 'clusterctl generate cluster' to create workload clusters. Verify with 'kubectl get clusters' and 'clusterctl describe cluster'.
codeSnippets: clusterctl generate cluster my-cluster --kubernetes-version v1.31.0 --control-plane-machine-count 1 --worker-machine-count 3 > my-cluster.yaml

prerequisites.description: An existing Kubernetes cluster (v1.28+) to serve as the management cluster. The clusterctl CLI must be installed.
